attribution.text: Images used in this document come from these sources.

links.context: The word háček means ‘little hook’ and it is one of the things that sets Czech apart from many other European languages. It is found on a number of letters we are already familiar with. However, these letters now produce entirely new sounds that need to be memorized as individual letters (they have their own place in the Czech alphabet).
Images used in this document come from these sources.

Pronunciation of p, t, and k

paragraphs.text: Pronunciation of p, t, and k
paragraphs.heading: Pronunciation of p, t, and k
paragraphs.nextText: In English the sounds p, t, and k are often pronounced with a puff of air following the articulation of the consonant sound. This is known as aspiration. The Czech sounds p, t, and k are not aspirated. Listen to the examples below and try to mimic their pronunciation.
paragraphs.contextText:
Pronunciation of p, t, and k
guláš ‘goulash’ (listen)
In English the sounds p, t, and k are often pronounced with a puff of air following the articulation of the consonant sound. This is known as aspiration. The Czech sounds p, t, and k are not aspirated. Listen to the examples below and try to mimic their pronunciation.

The sound ch is not found in English. Two strategies that I have found helpful:

paragraphs.text: The sound ch is not found in English. Two strategies that I have found helpful:
paragraphs.heading: The sound ch is not found in English. Two strategies that I have found helpful:
paragraphs.nextText: Start to pronounced a k sound. Now, if you do it slowly, you’ll feel that your tongue is pressed up against the top of the back of your mouth and then the tongue moves away from that area. Feel as your tongue moves away and try to hold it just in the position where it is barely touching but air can still flow by. This is how your tongue should be positioned.
paragraphs.contextText:

Czech has a rolled r. Some English speakers can pronounce it, while many others have difficulties. However, despite what you may have heard in the past, everyone CAN pronounce a rolled r with a little practice.
The sound of a rolled r is created by the trilling of the tip of the tongue. It’s vibrating really fast (almost like a hummingbird’s wings). However, students are often fooled into thinking that they have to do something with the tip of their tongue to make it move that fast. The fact of the matter is that we don’t have muscles in our tongue that can do that, so despite our efforts, we just can’t get it.

Czech has a rolled r. Some English speakers can pronounce it, while many others have difficulties. However, despite what you may have heard in the past, everyone CAN pronounce a rolled r with a little practice.
The sound of a rolled r is created by the trilling of the tip of the tongue. It’s vibrating really fast (almost like a hummingbird’s wings). However, students are often fooled into thinking that they have to do something with the tip of their tongue to make it move that fast. The fact of the matter is that we don’t have muscles in our tongue that can do that, so despite our efforts, we just can’t get it.
The secret – relax the tip of your tongue. Hold your tongue so the tip of it is just behind your teeth. Now exhale sharply! If your tongue is positioned correctly, the air should make your tongue begin to move. This will take some practice, but with time you should be able to get it.

paragraphs.heading: 1.5 - Česká příjmení – Czech Last Names
paragraphs.contextText:
1.5 - Česká příjmení – Czech Last Names
Male Last Names Like Nouns

paragraphs.heading: Male Last Names Like Nouns
paragraphs.nextText: In Czech, last names will have different forms based on gender.  For males, last names can have a few different forms.  Many male last names will end in a consonant like most masculine nouns such as Dvořák, Hájek, or Novák.  Male last names can also end in –a such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička.  These first two types look like nouns, and thus will be treated grammatically like nouns.  Despite the fact that names such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička end in an -a, they are still masculine last names.
paragraphs.contextText:
Male Last Names Like Nouns
1.5 - Česká příjmení – Czech Last Names
In Czech, last names will have different forms based on gender.  For males, last names can have a few different forms.  Many male last names will end in a consonant like most masculine nouns such as Dvořák, Hájek, or Novák.  Male last names can also end in –a such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička.  These first two types look like nouns, and thus will be treated grammatically like nouns.  Despite the fact that names such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička end in an -a, they are still masculine last names.

Male Last Names Like Nouns
In Czech, last names will have different forms based on gender.  For males, last names can have a few different forms.  Many male last names will end in a consonant like most masculine nouns such as Dvořák, Hájek, or Novák.  Male last names can also end in –a such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička.  These first two types look like nouns, and thus will be treated grammatically like nouns.  Despite the fact that names such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička end in an -a, they are still masculine last names.
Male Last Names Like Adjectives

paragraphs.heading: Male Last Names Like Adjectives
paragraphs.previousText: In Czech, last names will have different forms based on gender.  For males, last names can have a few different forms.  Many male last names will end in a consonant like most masculine nouns such as Dvořák, Hájek, or Novák.  Male last names can also end in –a such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička.  These first two types look like nouns, and thus will be treated grammatically like nouns.  Despite the fact that names such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička end in an -a, they are still masculine last names.
paragraphs.nextText: Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.
paragraphs.contextText:
Male Last Names Like Adjectives
In Czech, last names will have different forms based on gender.  For males, last names can have a few different forms.  Many male last names will end in a consonant like most masculine nouns such as Dvořák, Hájek, or Novák.  Male last names can also end in –a such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička.  These first two types look like nouns, and thus will be treated grammatically like nouns.  Despite the fact that names such as Svoboda, Kučera, or Růžička end in an -a, they are still masculine last names.
Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.

paragraphs.text: Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.
paragraphs.heading: Male Last Names Like Adjectives
paragraphs.contextText:
Male Last Names Like Adjectives
Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.
Female Last Names

paragraphs.heading: Female Last Names
paragraphs.previousText: Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.
paragraphs.nextText: Female last names, on the other hand, will all look like adjectives and will end with the letter –á.  They are derived from the corresponding male last name (based on the last name of the woman’s father or husband).  If the male variant of the last name looks like a noun, you will drop any final vowels (if the male name ends in -a, such as Kundera)  and add the ending –ová. Based on the male names above, a female last name would be Dvořáková, Nováková, Svobodová, or Růžičková. Moreover, if the masculine form of the last name ends in -ec, -ek, or -el, they drop the -e- from the name when adding -ová, so Hájek → Hájková, Němec → Němcová, Havel → Havlová.
paragraphs.contextText:
Female Last Names
Other male last names may end in –ý like a masculine adjective such as Novotný, Černý, or Pokorný.  Since these last names appear like adjectives, they will take adjective endings.
Female last names, on the other hand, will all look like adjectives and will end with the letter –á.  They are derived from the corresponding male last name (based on the last name of the woman’s father or husband).  If the male variant of the last name looks like a noun, you will drop any final vowels (if the male name ends in -a, such as Kundera)  and add the ending –ová. Based on the male names above, a female last name would be Dvořáková, Nováková, Svobodová, or Růžičková. Moreover, if the masculine form of the last name ends in -ec, -ek, or -el, they drop the -e- from the name when adding -ová, so Hájek → Hájková, Němec → Němcová, Havel → Havlová.

paragraphs.heading: 1.3 - Noun Gender
paragraphs.nextText: In this lesson you are going to learn about noun gender. Nouns are words that refer to people, places, things, ideas, etc. All nouns have a gender in Czech.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.3 - Noun Gender
In this lesson you are going to learn about noun gender. Nouns are words that refer to people, places, things, ideas, etc. All nouns have a gender in Czech.

paragraphs.text: In this lesson you are going to learn about noun gender. Nouns are words that refer to people, places, things, ideas, etc. All nouns have a gender in Czech.
paragraphs.heading: 1.3 - Noun Gender
paragraphs.nextText: We are somewhat familiar with this concept from English, e.g. - we have words like waiter and waitress, actor and actress, etc.  Knowing the gender of these nouns is easy and comes quite naturally and it is because of this that we call this natural gender. Similar forms exist in Czech (though they are much more widespread than in English). In Czech, the most common way of differentiating between a masculine and a feminine form is the suffix -ka, which is added to make many nouns feminine.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.3 - Noun Gender
In this lesson you are going to learn about noun gender. Nouns are words that refer to people, places, things, ideas, etc. All nouns have a gender in Czech.
We are somewhat familiar with this concept from English, e.g. - we have words like waiter and waitress, actor and actress, etc.  Knowing the gender of these nouns is easy and comes quite naturally and it is because of this that we call this natural gender. Similar forms exist in Czech (though they are much more widespread than in English). In Czech, the most common way of differentiating between a masculine and a feminine form is the suffix -ka, which is added to make many nouns feminine.

Grammatical Gender

paragraphs.heading: Grammatical Gender
paragraphs.nextText: In Czech, all nouns have gender, regardless of whether they refer to a human. For example, stůl ‘table’ is masculine, while tužka ‘pencil’ is feminine. There is very little rhyme or reason to this, and it is something that we just have to learn as we commit new words to memory. In Czech, the grammatical gender of a noun is virtually always the same as its natural gender, as in the case of the words listed above. For example, muž (m.), or žena (f.) are predictable by the meaning of the word. Czech nouns will be either masculine, feminine, or neuter, and you have to learn the gender of nouns as you go in order to use it properly. Take for example the following nouns:
paragraphs.contextText:
Grammatical Gender
‘writer (male and female)’

paragraphs.heading: 1.10 - Demonstratives - Plural Forms
paragraphs.nextText: Remember that these demonstrative pronouns are used to demonstrate a particular object/person, e.g. Those flowers are pretty. In this instance, people are just talking about a bunch of flowers. One of the speakers refers to a set of flowers as those flowers to distinguish it from all the other chairs they might have been talking about.

Unchanging to je…./to jsou…

paragraphs.heading: 1.10 - Demonstratives - Plural Forms
paragraphs.previousText: Remember that these demonstrative pronouns are used to demonstrate a particular object/person, e.g. Those flowers are pretty. In this instance, people are just talking about a bunch of flowers. One of the speakers refers to a set of flowers as those flowers to distinguish it from all the other chairs they might have been talking about.
paragraphs.nextText: Remember that whenever you are identifying objects, you just use to je…./to jsou… with unchanging to. In these instances you are not directly modifying the noun, i.e. you’re not saying those cats are fat - ty kočky jsou tlusté, but rather just identifying them, i.e. those are fat cats - to jsou tlusté kočky. To read up on this more, see 1.8 - Grammar Explanation - Unchanging to, part 2
paragraphs.contextText:
1.10 - Demonstratives - Plural Forms
Remember that these demonstrative pronouns are used to demonstrate a particular object/person, e.g. Those flowers are pretty. In this instance, people are just talking about a bunch of flowers. One of the speakers refers to a set of flowers as those flowers to distinguish it from all the other chairs they might have been talking about.
Unchanging to je…./to jsou…
Remember that whenever you are identifying objects, you just use to je…./to jsou… with unchanging to. In these instances you are not directly modifying the noun, i.e. you’re not saying those cats are fat - ty kočky jsou tlusté, but rather just identifying them, i.e. those are fat cats - to jsou tlusté kočky. To read up on this more, see 1.8 - Grammar Explanation - Unchanging to, part 2

paragraphs.heading: 1.2 - Names and Familiar Names
paragraphs.contextText:
1.2 - Names and Familiar Names
Česká jména – Czech first names

paragraphs.heading: Česká jména – Czech first names
paragraphs.nextText: Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.
paragraphs.contextText:
Česká jména – Czech first names
1.2 - Names and Familiar Names
Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.

paragraphs.text: Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.
paragraphs.heading: Česká jména – Czech first names
paragraphs.nextText: Other names such as Vojtěch or Anežka may seem more foreign to the native English speaker.  Many of these Czech names, however,  also have English counterparts.  Vojtěch translates to Adalbert (seriously, do you actually know anyone with this name in English?), while Anežka is the Czech equivalent of Agnes.  Some names may be only slightly different than their English versions such as the Czech male name Jan for John, or the female name Jana for Jane. Obviously some Czech names are going to be more popular than their English counterparts and vice-versa.

Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.
Other names such as Vojtěch or Anežka may seem more foreign to the native English speaker.  Many of these Czech names, however,  also have English counterparts.  Vojtěch translates to Adalbert (seriously, do you actually know anyone with this name in English?), while Anežka is the Czech equivalent of Agnes.  Some names may be only slightly different than their English versions such as the Czech male name Jan for John, or the female name Jana for Jane. Obviously some Czech names are going to be more popular than their English counterparts and vice-versa.

paragraphs.heading: Česká jména – Czech first names
paragraphs.previousText: Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.
paragraphs.nextText: Many Czech names are made up of Slavic roots that have meaning.  The name Jarmila comes from the words for ‘spring’ (jaro) and ‘to love’ (milovat), while the name Bohdan comes from the words for ‘God’ (bůh) and ‘to give’ (dát).  So Jarmila is one who loves the spring, and Bohdan is God-given.
paragraphs.contextText:
Česká jména – Czech first names
Many Czech names are similar to English names.  For example, Martin and Barbora are common Czech names that have obvious English equivalents. Some names might look a little foreign at first, such as Kateřina or Jan, but these are equivalent to English Catherine/Kathryn and John.
Other names such as Vojtěch or Anežka may seem more foreign to the native English speaker.  Many of these Czech names, however,  also have English counterparts.  Vojtěch translates to Adalbert (seriously, do you actually know anyone with this name in English?), while Anežka is the Czech equivalent of Agnes.  Some names may be only slightly different than their English versions such as the Czech male name Jan for John, or the female name Jana for Jane. Obviously some Czech names are going to be more popular than their English counterparts and vice-versa.
Many Czech names are made up of Slavic roots that have meaning.  The name Jarmila comes from the words for ‘spring’ (jaro) and ‘to love’ (milovat), while the name Bohdan comes from the words for ‘God’ (bůh) and ‘to give’ (dát).  So Jarmila is one who loves the spring, and Bohdan is God-given.

Czech Familiar Names

paragraphs.heading: Czech Familiar Names
paragraphs.previousText: Many Czech names are made up of Slavic roots that have meaning.  The name Jarmila comes from the words for ‘spring’ (jaro) and ‘to love’ (milovat), while the name Bohdan comes from the words for ‘God’ (bůh) and ‘to give’ (dát).  So Jarmila is one who loves the spring, and Bohdan is God-given.
paragraphs.nextText: Czech makes frequent use of familiar name forms (i.e. nicknames).  Many of these names will simply be shorter forms of the first name.  It is similar to English Danny for Daniel or Drew for Andrew.  Some Czech nicknames will actually be longer than their original name such as the nickname Jirka for the Czech equivalent to George – Jiří, or Hanka as a nickname for Hana.  In fact, some Czech nicknames don’t look at all like the name from which they were derived such as the nickname Pepa for the Czech name Josef/Jozef.
paragraphs.contextText:
Czech Familiar Names
Many Czech names are made up of Slavic roots that have meaning.  The name Jarmila comes from the words for ‘spring’ (jaro) and ‘to love’ (milovat), while the name Bohdan comes from the words for ‘God’ (bůh) and ‘to give’ (dát).  So Jarmila is one who loves the spring, and Bohdan is God-given.
Czech makes frequent use of familiar name forms (i.e. nicknames).  Many of these names will simply be shorter forms of the first name.  It is similar to English Danny for Daniel or Drew for Andrew.  Some Czech nicknames will actually be longer than their original name such as the nickname Jirka for the Czech equivalent to George – Jiří, or Hanka as a nickname for Hana.  In fact, some Czech nicknames don’t look at all like the name from which they were derived such as the nickname Pepa for the Czech name Josef/Jozef.

paragraphs.heading: Czech has the following demonstrative pronoun forms:
paragraphs.nextText: The forms ten, ta, to are used primarily in the meaning “that” or “the”. They are placed before the noun or adjective + noun that they modify, e.g. ten počítač ‘that computer’, ta lampa ‘that lamp’, or to pivo ‘that beer’.
paragraphs.contextText:
Czech has the following demonstrative pronoun forms:
ta
to
The forms ten, ta, to are used primarily in the meaning “that” or “the”. They are placed before the noun or adjective + noun that they modify, e.g. ten počítač ‘that computer’, ta lampa ‘that lamp’, or to pivo ‘that beer’.

paragraphs.text: The forms ten, ta, to are used primarily in the meaning “that” or “the”. They are placed before the noun or adjective + noun that they modify, e.g. ten počítač ‘that computer’, ta lampa ‘that lamp’, or to pivo ‘that beer’.
paragraphs.heading: Czech has the following demonstrative pronoun forms:
paragraphs.contextText:
Czech has the following demonstrative pronoun forms:
to
The forms ten, ta, to are used primarily in the meaning “that” or “the”. They are placed before the noun or adjective + noun that they modify, e.g. ten počítač ‘that computer’, ta lampa ‘that lamp’, or to pivo ‘that beer’.
Ten stůl je velmi starý.

-hle ‘this one (right here)’

paragraphs.heading: -hle ‘this one (right here)’
paragraphs.nextText: If you want to make it really specific that you are referring to something right here, i.e. ‘this (right here)’, add -hle to the forms ten, ta, to (i.e. tenhle, tahle, tohle).
paragraphs.contextText:
-hle ‘this one (right here)’
That beer is good.
If you want to make it really specific that you are referring to something right here, i.e. ‘this (right here)’, add -hle to the forms ten, ta, to (i.e. tenhle, tahle, tohle).

paragraphs.nextText: In the previous section, we learned the forms ten, ta, to as the masculine, feminine, and neuter forms of the demonstrative. Whenever you are saying that professor is... or that chair is..., you will use the forms ten, ta, to which match the gender of the noun that follows.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.7 - Unchanging to vs. Demonstrative ten, ta, to
In the previous section, we learned the forms ten, ta, to as the masculine, feminine, and neuter forms of the demonstrative. Whenever you are saying that professor is... or that chair is..., you will use the forms ten, ta, to which match the gender of the noun that follows.

paragraphs.contextText:
1.6 - Božena Němcová and Babička / Grandmother
Božena Němcová (4 February 1820 in Vienna – 21 January 1862 in Prague) was a Czech writer of the final phase of the so-called Czech National Revival movement, in which the Czech legends and folklore played an integral part.
In her childhood, Němcová lived near the small town of Ratibořice, in northeast Bohemia, where her grandmother Magdalena Novotná played an important part in her life. Němcová would later write her most famous novel, Grandmother (Babička) with the main character inspired by her grandmother, and the castle in Ratibořice is also featured in the novel.

Němcová would later write her most famous novel, Grandmother (Babička) with the main character inspired by her grandmother, and the castle in Ratibořice is also featured in the novel.
paragraphs.heading: 1.6 - Božena Němcová and Babička / Grandmother
paragraphs.previousText: Božena Němcová (4 February 1820 in Vienna – 21 January 1862 in Prague) was a Czech writer of the final phase of the so-called Czech National Revival movement, in which the Czech legends and folklore played an integral part.
paragraphs.nextText: When she was 17 years old, she married Josef Němec, a state employee, with whom Němcová is said to have had strained relations. Eventually, Němcová relocated to Prague, where she connected with other authors and poets of the time. Němcová lived there for the remainder of her life, and when she died, was already something of a national hero.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.6 - Božena Němcová and Babička / Grandmother
Božena Němcová (4 February 1820 in Vienna – 21 January 1862 in Prague) was a Czech writer of the final phase of the so-called Czech National Revival movement, in which the Czech legends and folklore played an integral part.
In her childhood, Němcová lived near the small town of Ratibořice, in northeast Bohemia, where her grandmother Magdalena Novotná played an important part in her life. Němcová would later write her most famous novel, Grandmother (Babička) with the main character inspired by her grandmother, and the castle in Ratibořice is also featured in the novel.
When she was 17 years old, she married Josef Němec, a state employee, with whom Němcová is said to have had strained relations. Eventually, Němcová relocated to Prague, where she connected with other authors and poets of the time. Němcová lived there for the remainder of her life, and when she died, was already something of a national hero.

paragraphs.nextText: Her image can be found on the 500 Czech crown note (about $20), distinguishing the Czech Republic from the United States by featuring a woman—and an author!—on its currency.

1.6 - Božena Němcová and Babička / Grandmother
In her childhood, Němcová lived near the small town of Ratibořice, in northeast Bohemia, where her grandmother Magdalena Novotná played an important part in her life. Němcová would later write her most famous novel, Grandmother (Babička) with the main character inspired by her grandmother, and the castle in Ratibořice is also featured in the novel.
When she was 17 years old, she married Josef Němec, a state employee, with whom Němcová is said to have had strained relations. Eventually, Němcová relocated to Prague, where she connected with other authors and poets of the time. Němcová lived there for the remainder of her life, and when she died, was already something of a national hero.
Her image can be found on the 500 Czech crown note (about $20), distinguishing the Czech Republic from the United States by featuring a woman—and an author!—on its currency.

When she was 17 years old, she married Josef Němec, a state employee, with whom Němcová is said to have had strained relations. Eventually, Němcová relocated to Prague, where she connected with other authors and poets of the time. Němcová lived there for the remainder of her life, and when she died, was already something of a national hero.
Her image can be found on the 500 Czech crown note (about $20), distinguishing the Czech Republic from the United States by featuring a woman—and an author!—on its currency.
(adapted from Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bo%C5%BEena_N%C4%9Bmcov%C3%A1)

paragraphs.nextText: It is easy to figure out whether an adjective is hard or soft by looking at its form in dictionaries or vocabulary lists. If it ends in -ý in the masculine singular, it is a hard stem adjective. If it ends in -í, it is a soft adjective. When you learn an adjective, you have to learn whether an adjective is hard or soft. It will then follow that specific pattern listed above.

paragraphs.text: It is easy to figure out whether an adjective is hard or soft by looking at its form in dictionaries or vocabulary lists. If it ends in -ý in the masculine singular, it is a hard stem adjective. If it ends in -í, it is a soft adjective. When you learn an adjective, you have to learn whether an adjective is hard or soft. paragraphs.heading: Soft Adjective Endings
paragraphs.nextText: Two other soft adjectives that you have so far encountered are domácí ‘(of or related to) home’ and psací ‘(used for) writing’. Domácí úkol is a ‘home task’ (úkol ‘task’) and psací stůl is a ‘writing table’. Other examples of soft adjectives are inteligentní ‘intelligent’ or hlavní ‘main’, such as hlavní nádraží ‘main train station’.

paragraphs.text: Two other soft adjectives that you have so far encountered are domácí ‘(of or related to) home’ and psací ‘(used for) writing’. Domácí úkol is a ‘home task’ (úkol ‘task’) and psací stůl is a ‘writing table’. Other examples of soft adjectives are inteligentní ‘intelligent’ or hlavní ‘main’, such as hlavní nádraží ‘main train station’.
paragraphs.heading: Soft Adjective Endings
paragraphs.previousText: It is easy to figure out whether an adjective is hard or soft by looking at its form in dictionaries or vocabulary lists. If it ends in -ý in the masculine singular, it is a hard stem adjective. If it ends in -í, it is a soft adjective. When you learn an adjective, you have to learn whether an adjective is hard or soft. It will then follow that specific pattern listed above.

paragraphs.contextText:
Soft Adjective Endings
It is easy to figure out whether an adjective is hard or soft by looking at its form in dictionaries or vocabulary lists. If it ends in -ý in the masculine singular, it is a hard stem adjective. If it ends in -í, it is a soft adjective. When you learn an adjective, you have to learn whether an adjective is hard or soft. It will then follow that specific pattern listed above.
Two other soft adjectives that you have so far encountered are domácí ‘(of or related to) home’ and psací ‘(used for) writing’. Domácí úkol is a ‘home task’ (úkol ‘task’) and psací stůl is a ‘writing table’. Other examples of soft adjectives are inteligentní ‘intelligent’ or hlavní ‘main’, such as hlavní nádraží ‘main train station’.

Jak se řekne __________?

paragraphs.heading: Jak se řekne __________?

paragraphs.nextText: ‘How do you say bathroom, I’m hungry, castle, bus ticket…’ There are going to be numerous occasions when you want to ask this question.

Jak se řekne __________?
You’ve just started with Czech and you’re going to understandably get into a number of situations where you will want to know how to say something or don’t know what something means. Two phrases will come in handy:
‘How do you say bathroom, I’m hungry, castle, bus ticket…’ There are going to be numerous occasions when you want to ask this question.

1.1C - Pozdravy - Form small groups (2-4 people) and practice introducing yourself and each other. First imagine that you are all dignitaries at a special event in Prague. Introduce yourselves to each other formally. Then, pretend that you are all meeting for a new student club and introduce yourselves informally. Finally, practice introducing some members of your group to members of other groups.
Alternative - form a circle and-Dobrý den, já jsem Michael.
-Dobrý den, já jsem Anna / jmenuju se Anna.
-Dobrý den, já jsem Tomáš / jmenuju se Tomáš.
-Těší mě [mně].
-Mě taky / Těší mě taky
play a chain game. Each student introduces him/herself and then the students that have already introduced themselves. Whoever can remember the longest chain wins.
-Ahoj, já jsem Bára.
-Ahoj, já jsem Lukáš a to je Bára.
-Ahoj, já jsem Lucie a to je Lukáš a Bára.
-Těší mě. Mě taky!
Formal Greetings
Dobrý den - Hello
Na shledanou - Goodbye
Informal Greetings
Ahoj, Nazdar, Čau - Hi
Ahoj, Nazdar, Čau - Bye
Introduction Phrases
Já jsem... - I am...
To je... - This is…
Těší mě - Pleased to meet you.
Mě taky - Pleased to meet you too.

__atoh

1.1E - Pronunciation - Practice in pairs pronouncing the following words in Czech. When you are sure you are pronouncing it correctly, ask your teacher to check you. If both members of a team pronounce the word correctly, they receive a point. The team with the most points at the end wins!
auto
Čech
chata
kočka
zahrádka
Češka
papír
kolej
hrad [hrat]
moře
holka
učitel
autobus
houba
židle
1.1F - What does __________ mean? How do you say __________ ? - Useful phrases to ask about the meanings of words.
Let’s say you want to say something in Czech but don’t know what Czech words you need. Use the phrase Jak se řekne česky __________, which literally means ‘How is __________ said in Czech?’. For each of the English words on the left, ask a partner how it is said in Czech. Work together to match the English words on the left with their Czech meanings on the right.
car
jídlo
dog
mapa
dorm
kolej
friend
auto
castle
pes
map
hrad
forest
pivo
park
kamarád(ka)
food
les
beer
park
Now let’s imagine you run into some Czech words and you don’t know what they mean in English. Use the phrase Co znamená __________? which means ‘What does __________ mean?’
The words below are ones that you haven’t probably encountered yet. With a partner, take turns asking each other for the meaning of these words. Use the dictionary at http://slovnik.lingea.cz/, the Android or iOS app for this dictionary, or another reference work to find these words. Then match them with the pictures on the right.
rybník

les

bouřka

káva

kapr

Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.
Jak se řekne česky…? -
‘How is __________ said in Czech?’
Řekne se to…
‘It is (lit. it is said…)’
Co znamená __________?
‘What does __________ mean?’
Znamená to __________.
‘It means __________.’
1.1G - Na shledanou! Ahoj! Čau! Nazdar! - Watch the videos below and practice pronouncing the Czech goodbyes with your instructor. Pay particular attention to copying the intonation that Czech speakers use.

Formal - Na shledanou!

Informal - Ahoj! Čau! Nazdar!
Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.

1.2A - Vocabulary Review - Nouns – Use Quizlet Live in class to drill your knowledge of the nouns we have learned.
Live
Live
1.2B - Co budeme potřebovat? - Imagine you are in the following scenarios. List out some items you might need in Czech.
a study group before a test
a party after final exams
moving into a new apartment
vacation in the Czech countryside
meeting up with friends in a park
1.2C - Mimozemšťané! - Aliens have landed. Obviously their first goal was to learn Czech(!) and have learned at least enough Czech to ask simple questions about people and things. With a partner practice asking about and identifying the following objects.
Part A - Use this slideshow to practice as a class.

Part B - Now work with a partner to identify the objects below.

Co to je?

Co to je?








Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.
Co to je? -What is this?
To je… -This is (a)...
1.2D - Pronunciation - Practice in pairs pronouncing the following words in Czech. When you are sure you are pronouncing them correctly, ask your teacher to check you. If both members of a team pronounce a word correctly, they receive a point. The team with the most points at the end wins!
město
letiště
televize
jméno
pes
divadlo
náměstí
paní
umění
pěkný
velký
špinavá
hlavní
šedý
tady
nezapomeň! - don’t forget!
d, t, n + i/í or ě are pronounced ď, ť, ň
p, b, f, v, m + ě are pronounced [pje, bje, fje, vje, mně]
1.2E - Jména - Take a look at the pictures in the chart below. With a partner ask who each of the people are. By yourself or with a partner come up with Czech names for them.






Now, introduce these people to your class. Try to add some extra details about them, e.g. je učitelka ‘she’s a teacher’ or je student ‘he’s a student’
Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.
kdo to je? - ‘who is this/that?’
To je - ‘this is (a)...’

1.3A - Forms Review - být ‘to be’ – Use Quizlet Live in class to drill your knowledge of the nouns we have learned.
Live
Live
1.3B - být ‘to be’. Write out the forms of the verb být on scraps of paper. On the other side of the piece of paper write the forms of the personal pronouns (já, ty, …). Practice with a partner recalling the forms of the verb without looking at a chart.
the verb být ‘to be’
(já) jsem
(my) jsme
(ty) jsi
(vy) jste
(on, ona, ono) je
(oni) jsou
1.3C - Introductions. Fill out the chart below then walking around the classroom share your responses with your other classmates. Try to look gradually less and less at your notes below, practicing these introductions several times.
Já jsem ____________________________ (name)
Jsem ______________________________ (profession)
After you have talked with everyone, with a partner or your instructor go back and review the names of everyone you talked to, e.g. to je Pavel, to je Lucie…
student / studentka
učitel / učitelka
profesor / profesorka

Lucie - Nazdar! Já __________ Lucie.
David - Ahoj! __________ studentka?
Lucie - Ano, __________. Co ty? __________ taky student?
David - Ne, nejsem.

1.3G - Prosím - In pairs or small groups come up with two short skits using some of the polite phrases we have learned (see margin notes). Since your vocabulary and grammar are limited, use of gesture and pantomime are perfectly acceptable here.
Skit 1 - Create a skit in which someone in either a formal or informal situation requests something.
Skit 2 - Create a skit in which someone apologizes for something.
Useful for Requests
děkuju/děkuji - thank you
díky/dík - thanks
není zač - you're welcome
prosím- please, you’re welcome
Useful for Apologies or Cautioning
promiňte - excuse me, sorry
pozor - be careful

nezapomeňte! - don’t forget!
to je -  ‘this is...’

The word tohle means ‘this thing (right here)’. It is used particularly when you are close of an object.

Josef Mathauser - Kněžna Libuše věští slávu Prahy
Names of some of the major figures in the old Czech legends -

Praotec Čech
Krok - ruler of a Bohemian tribe

Svatopluk a jeho synové (Mojmír II., Svatopluk II. a Predslav)

1.5D - Kreslení - Your instructor will pull out two cards. The first card will have a vocabulary item from this unit. The second will have a color vocabulary word. Everyone will race to draw that particular item in the color. When you are finished, hold up your picture and call out what it is in Czech.
To je modré auto!

To je fialová a zelená houba!

Je to starý červený telefon.

Tomáš je spisovatel a Lenka je učitelka.

1.6A - Vocative - Imagine you are in a room with a lot of people and are trying to get the attention of several friends. How would you call out to them? Remember, when calling someone’s name, you need to put their name into the vocative case. Use this optional presentation to practice names as a group and then complete the names below with a partner.

1.7E - Jaký je ten dům? 'what kind' - jaký? jaká? jaké? – Describe the pictures below using adjectives you have learned. For even more slides and to complete as a group, use this presentation.
1.8B - Co to je? Kdo to je? - Ask each other about the following objects/people in the pictures below and identify them. Use the pictures below, or this presentation if completing as a group.

1.9D - Procházka Prahou / A Walk through Prague - Now watch through this video as a class. As you are watching, call out the things you see. Try to be descriptive, i.e. you don’t just se restaurace but České restaurace. Point out what you see in class. Then optionally rewatch the video and ask in Czech (jak se řekne _______) how to name various things you don’t know in Czech yet.
Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.

1.9E - Praha z dronu - Watch the following video of Prague and point out anything that you can say in Czech. Be as descriptive as possible, using as many adjectives as you can think of to describe the things you are seeing. Then optionally rewatch the video and ask in Czech how to name various things you don’t know in Czech yet.
Images used in this exercise are taken from sources listed here.
1.9F - Vaše město z dronu nebo timelapse - Search on websites such as https://youtube.com or https://vimeo.com for timelapse or drone videos of your city. Practice naming as many things as you can.

1.10B - Barvení - Each person in the class will receive all of the cards below. You can download a separate copy of them here. For each of the rows, color all of the objects a single color (e.g. all cars are red, all fish are yellow, etc.). Then make statements about them to a partner. At the end compare yours with the rest of the class.

paragraphs.nextText: The word tohle means ‘this thing (right here)’. It is used particularly when you are close of an object.

In fact, the form that you find after the word z ‘from’ is known as the Genitive case. We won’t be spending too much time on these forms now, but it is worth knowing that words will sometimes change their endings in Czech in situations where we wouldn’t change the way a word ends in English.

paragraphs.nextText: 1.11B - Odkud pocházejí? You probably noticed that some of the speakers used the verb pocházet this word is just another way of saying where you are from, in the same way that in English you can say either “Where are you from?” or “Where do you come from?”. Now using the verb pocházet ‘to come from’, say where each of the people comes from.

paragraphs.heading: 1.9 Adjectives - Nominative Plural
paragraphs.nextText: In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.9 Adjectives - Nominative Plural
In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.

paragraphs.text: In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.
paragraphs.heading: 1.9 Adjectives - Nominative Plural
paragraphs.contextText:
1.9 Adjectives - Nominative Plural
In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.
Masculine Nouns: Animate vs. Inanimate

paragraphs.heading: Masculine Nouns: Animate vs. Inanimate
paragraphs.previousText: In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.
paragraphs.nextText: As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.
paragraphs.contextText:
Masculine Nouns: Animate vs. Inanimate
In this lesson we are going to learn a new set of adjective endings to be used when adjectives are paired with nouns that are plural. There will be different endings depending on the gender of the noun, as explained in the following subsections.
As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.

paragraphs.text: As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.
paragraphs.heading: Masculine Nouns: Animate vs. Inanimate
paragraphs.contextText:
Masculine Nouns: Animate vs. Inanimate
As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.
Masculine Inanimate Adjective Endings

paragraphs.heading: Masculine Inanimate Adjective Endings
paragraphs.previousText: As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.
paragraphs.nextText: Adjectives modifying masculine inanimate nouns have plural forms -é for hard-stem adjectives,  and -í for soft stem adjectives. Important: pay attention to what pattern the adjective follows (hard or soft). Hard-stem adjectives can be paired with soft-stem nouns and vice-versa; so one can say nové počítače ‘new computers’ or moderní stoly ‘modern tables’.
paragraphs.contextText:
Masculine Inanimate Adjective Endings
As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.
Adjectives modifying masculine inanimate nouns have plural forms -é for hard-stem adjectives,  and -í for soft stem adjectives. Important: pay attention to what pattern the adjective follows (hard or soft). Hard-stem adjectives can be paired with soft-stem nouns and vice-versa; so one can say nové počítače ‘new computers’ or moderní stoly ‘modern tables’.

Hard-stem adjectives can be paired with soft-stem nouns and vice-versa; so one can say nové počítače ‘new computers’ or moderní stoly ‘modern tables’.

The adjective ending -í will also cause some consonant mutations,

hyperlinks.context: As with nouns in the plural, there are going to be different endings for adjectives modifying animate and inanimate masculine nouns. The endings for adjectives paired with masculine animate nouns are a little more complicated, so we will cover them last.

The plural endings for masculine animate adjectives are -í for hard-stem and -í for soft-stem. We recall from our discussion of masculine animate nouns that the ending -i caused some consonant mutations (kluk ‘boy’ → kluci ‘boys’, Čech ‘a Czech’ → Češi  ‘Czechs’, etc.). The adjective ending -í will also cause some consonant mutations,

paragraphs.heading: 1.8 - Noun Stems (Hard and Soft)
paragraphs.nextText: In this lesson you will learn about nouns stems. Noun stems are the form of the noun that we will add endings to, for example to make them plural. Additionally, you will learn about the difference between hard and soft stem nouns. Nouns in Czech are either hard or soft stem. It is important to learn this distinction, because the endings hard- and soft-stem nouns will use different endings.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.8 - Noun Stems (Hard and Soft)
In this lesson you will learn about nouns stems. Noun stems are the form of the noun that we will add endings to, for example to make them plural. Additionally, you will learn about the difference between hard and soft stem nouns. Nouns in Czech are either hard or soft stem. It is important to learn this distinction, because the endings hard- and soft-stem nouns will use different endings.

1.8 - Noun Stems (Hard and Soft)
In this lesson you will learn about nouns stems. Noun stems are the form of the noun that we will add endings to, for example to make them plural. Additionally, you will learn about the difference between hard and soft stem nouns. Nouns in Czech are either hard or soft stem. It is important to learn this distinction, because the endings hard- and soft-stem nouns will use different endings.
Essentially, this lesson is about teaching you how to recognize different patterns for nouns, because we will need to know this when we make them plural (hard stems will take different endings than soft stems). We encountered something similar when we learned the difference between hard and soft adjectives.

Determine whether the stem is hard-stem or soft-stem. The stem type of a noun is only partially predictable and in some instances you will have to memorize whether a noun is soft stem or hard stem.  Here are some rules to help you determine the stem type:
Hard Stem

paragraphs.heading: Hard Stem
paragraphs.nextText: While we could give you a list of consonants that are hard-stem, it would be a long list that would be a pain to memorize. Instead, it’s best to define them in terms of the soft stem. If you have a masculine noun that is not soft stem (see below), then it’s hard stem. Some examples of hard-stem nouns are profesor, koncert, mobil, park, hrad. After you look at the soft-stem rules below, this list will make more sense.
paragraphs.contextText:
Hard Stem

The suffix -tel

Feminine Nouns

paragraphs.heading: Feminine Nouns
paragraphs.previousText: We have learned two words so far that end in -tel: přítel and učitel. Both of these words are soft stem nouns, and all masculine animate nouns that end in -tel are soft. This is something you just have to memorize.
paragraphs.nextText: It’s pretty simple to determine whether feminine nouns are hard or soft stem if you look at the noun. Before you take off the final vowel to determine the stem, you can already tell that these are hard-stem nouns.
paragraphs.contextText:
Feminine Nouns
We have learned two words so far that end in -tel: přítel and učitel. Both of these words are soft stem nouns, and all masculine animate nouns that end in -tel are soft. This is something you just have to memorize.
It’s pretty simple to determine whether feminine nouns are hard or soft stem if you look at the noun. Before you take off the final vowel to determine the stem, you can already tell that these are hard-stem nouns.

paragraphs.heading: The verb být ‘to be’
paragraphs.nextText: The word být in Czech means 'to be'. The form být is the infinitive of the verb, which basically translates as the 'to _______' form of a verb. Infinitives generally end in the letter -t in Czech. Some other examples of infinitives are dělat 'to do', rozumět 'to understand', pracovat 'to work'. Whenever we learn a new verb, we'll not only learn the infinitive, but also how the verb conjugates.

paragraphs.nextText: In order to negate the verb (i.e. I am not, you are not, etc.), you simply place ne in front of the conjugated forms. These are written as the same word. Did you notice the change in word order? In the question, the word order is inverted. This is fairly common in Czech and is the basic word order for statements and questions.

paragraphs.text: Did you notice the change in word order? In the question, the word order is inverted. This is fairly common in Czech and is the basic word order for statements and questions.
paragraphs.heading: 1.3 - Statements and Questions
paragraphs.nextText: However, Czech has somewhat freer word order in English. So how can you tell? Well, intonation plays a pretty big role. Statements tend to have a falling intonation, while questions tend to have rising intonation.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.3 - Statements and Questions
(audio)
Did you notice the change in word order? In the question, the word order is inverted. This is fairly common in Czech and is the basic word order for statements and questions.
However, Czech has somewhat freer word order in English. So how can you tell? Well, intonation plays a pretty big role. Statements tend to have a falling intonation, while questions tend to have rising intonation.

paragraphs.heading: 1. lekce - Vítejte! / Unit 1 - Welcome to Czech
paragraphs.nextText: In this unit you will be introduced to the very basics of the Czech language, from pronunciation to identifying objects, and describing people and things. Below you will find some more in-depth information about the topics covered in this unit.

In this unit you will be introduced to the very basics of the Czech language, from pronunciation to identifying objects, and describing people and things. Below you will find some more in-depth information about the topics covered in this unit.

links.context: We recall from the discussion above that masculine animate nouns are those which refer to humans or animals. The ending for masculine animate nouns is -i, regardless of whether the noun is hard or soft. However, recall from learning the pronunciation rules in 1.2 - Soft Pronunciations that certain consonants were pronounced differently in combination with -i. The plural of kamarád ‘friend’ is kamarádi ‘friends’ and the plural of student ‘student’ is studenti ‘students’. The d and t in these words is pronounced as ť and ď , respectively, because of the pronunciation rule. This is a type of consonant mutation (a change in consonants based on an ending). The ending -i causes some other mutations, based on the following rules ( k →c, h → z, ch → š, r → ř)

Masculine inanimate nouns form their plural by adding one of two endings, depending on whether the noun is hard stem or soft stem (see Czech noun stems). Hard stem nouns will add the ending -y, while soft stem nouns will add the ending -e.
Images used in this document come from these sources.

paragraphs.heading: 1.8 - Making Nouns Plural
paragraphs.nextText: In this lesson you will learn how to make things plural in Czech. In English, making a noun plural is relatively simple and is achieved by putting the ending -s or -es (cats, dishes). The rules for forming the plural in Czech are a bit more complex, but not too difficult to master if you pay attention to a few rules. First, here is a table with endings:

Masculine Animate Nouns

paragraphs.heading: Masculine Animate Nouns
paragraphs.nextText: We recall from the discussion above that masculine animate nouns are those which refer to humans or animals. The ending for masculine animate nouns is -i, regardless of whether the noun is hard or soft. However, recall from learning the pronunciation rules in 1.2 - Soft Pronunciations that certain consonants were pronounced differently in combination with -i. The plural of kamarád ‘friend’ is kamarádi ‘friends’ and the plural of student ‘student’ is studenti ‘students’. The d and t in these words is pronounced as ť and ď , respectively, because of the pronunciation rule. This is a type of consonant mutation (a change in consonants based on an ending). The ending -i causes some other mutations, based on the following rules ( k →c, h → z, ch → š, r → ř)
paragraphs.contextText:

paragraphs.nextText: Cvičení 1 –  Write a mini-dialogue (two-lines) showing how the people pictured might greet or say goodbye to each other.  Include both a greeting and the person's name, nickname, or profession in the vocative case. In the case of professions, you can often just use a form of pan or paní + the name of the profession, e.g. - pan doktor, literally 'Mr. Doctor'

paragraphs.heading: 1.10 - Colloquial Pronunciation (ý → ej)
paragraphs.previousText: In the Radio Praha story Not So Golden Oldies, we encountered this phrase. But wait, what kind of ending on that adjective is -ej? Isn’t the ending we learned -ý?

1.10 - Colloquial Pronunciation (ý → ej)
In the Radio Praha story Not So Golden Oldies, we encountered this phrase. But wait, what kind of ending on that adjective is -ej? Isn’t the ending we learned -ý?
Turns out it’s both. In colloquial Czech in Bohemia and some parts of Moravia (see map below), you tend to get -ej- where the standard language has -ý-. So you’ll run into things like:
velkej byt (instead of velký byt)

Czech Regions
The following explanation is meant to help with some of the colloquial dialect forms we’ll be encountering all throughout Czech class. You will hardly go much time in country or watching movies/television without hearing these dialectal differences.
In the colloquial language of Bohemian and some Moravian speakers (see map below) the sound ý is frequently pronounced as ej. The graphic below (source: https://syd.korpus.cz/) depicts the distribution of pronunciations být ‘to be’(Standard Czech) and bejt ‘to be (colloquial)’ (Colloquial Czech). As you can see, for a large portion of the Czech Republic, speakers will typically say ej instead of ý.

links.context: We will go through both men’s and women’s names. Note that men’s and women’s names largely follow the rules given in 1.3 - Grammar explanation - noun gender.

We will start by learning the vocative of names ending in -a. These are typically women’s names, though there are a few men’s names (such as Jirka, which is short for Jiří, or Honza, which is short for Jan – see 1.2 - Names and Familiar Names) as well. Names ending in -a have the vocative ending -o.
Images used in this document come from these sources.

paragraphs.heading: 1.6 - The Vocative Case
paragraphs.nextText: The vocative in Czech is the form of a person’s name that is used when you call out to them. Typical situations in which you would use the vocative include trying to get someone’s attention, saying hi, or calling on someone.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.6 - The Vocative Case
The vocative in Czech is the form of a person’s name that is used when you call out to them. Typical situations in which you would use the vocative include trying to get someone’s attention, saying hi, or calling on someone.

pan, paní, Last Names and Adjectives

paragraphs.text: pan, paní, Last Names and Adjectives
paragraphs.heading: pan, paní, Last Names and Adjectives
paragraphs.nextText: The vocative of pan is pane, while the vocative of paní is paní (i.e. no change). When calling on someone using pan or paní + last name, it is usually sufficient to keep the last name in the nominative form, i.e. not change it into the vocative.
paragraphs.contextText:
pan, paní, Last Names and Adjectives

The vocative of pan is pane, while the vocative of paní is paní (i.e. no change). When calling on someone using pan or paní + last name, it is usually sufficient to keep the last name in the nominative form, i.e. not change it into the vocative.

links.context: Antonín Leopold Dvořák (8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was of just about the same generation as Jirásek, a Czech composer who also had an interest in folk myths and song. After Bedřich Smetana, he was the second Czech composer to achieve worldwide recognition. Following Smetana's nationalist example, Dvořák frequently employed aspects, specifically rhythms, of the folk music of Moravia and his native Bohemia.

In 1896 once back in Prague, Dvořák wrote the symphonic poem The Water Sprite (Vodník), adapted from a poem of the same name in Karel Jaromír Erben’s celebrated collection, Kytice. You can listen to the opus on YouTube here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tevnw2zRDk0
a film adaptation of Kytice from 2000
a comics version of the poem Komiksová kytice (Transmedialist 2016)
First two paragraphs adapted from Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k)

paragraphs.text: Antonín Leopold Dvořák (8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was of just about the same generation as Jirásek, a Czech composer who also had an interest in folk myths and song. After Bedřich Smetana, he was the second Czech composer to achieve worldwide recognition. Following Smetana's nationalist example, Dvořák frequently employed aspects, specifically rhythms, of the folk music of Moravia and his native Bohemia.
paragraphs.heading: 1.8 - Antonín Dvořák and Jaromír Erben
paragraphs.nextText: In 1891 Dvořák was appointed as a professor at the Prague Conservatory, and a year later, in 1892, Dvořák moved to the United States and became the director of the National Conservatory of Music of America in New York City (where he remained with his family until 1985). While in the United States, Dvořák wrote his two most successful orchestral works. The Symphony From the New World spread his reputation worldwide. (The piece has become so emblematic of American life that the New York Philharmonic chose to perform it when they played in North Korea in 2008.) Dvořák also wrote his American String Quartet while in the U.S., his most appreciated piece of chamber music.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.8 - Antonín Dvořák and Jaromír Erben
Antonín Leopold Dvořák (8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was of just about the same generation as Jirásek, a Czech composer who also had an interest in folk myths and song. After Bedřich Smetana, he was the second Czech composer to achieve worldwide recognition. Following Smetana's nationalist example, Dvořák frequently employed aspects, specifically rhythms, of the folk music of Moravia and his native Bohemia.
In 1891 Dvořák was appointed as a professor at the Prague Conservatory, and a year later, in 1892, Dvořák moved to the United States and became the director of the National Conservatory of Music of America in New York City (where he remained with his family until 1985). While in the United States, Dvořák wrote his two most successful orchestral works. The Symphony From the New World spread his reputation worldwide. (The piece has become so emblematic of American life that the New York Philharmonic chose to perform it when they played in North Korea in 2008.) Dvořák also wrote his American String Quartet while in the U.S., his most appreciated piece of chamber music.

Antonín Leopold Dvořák (8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was of just about the same generation as Jirásek, a Czech composer who also had an interest in folk myths and song. After Bedřich Smetana, he was the second Czech composer to achieve worldwide recognition. Following Smetana's nationalist example, Dvořák frequently employed aspects, specifically rhythms, of the folk music of Moravia and his native Bohemia.
In 1891 Dvořák was appointed as a professor at the Prague Conservatory, and a year later, in 1892, Dvořák moved to the United States and became the director of the National Conservatory of Music of America in New York City (where he remained with his family until 1985). While in the United States, Dvořák wrote his two most successful orchestral works. The Symphony From the New World spread his reputation worldwide. (The piece has become so emblematic of American life that the New York Philharmonic chose to perform it when they played in North Korea in 2008.) Dvořák also wrote his American String Quartet while in the U.S., his most appreciated piece of chamber music.

CC-BY-SA
Images used in this document come from these sources.

hyperlinks.context: Antonín Leopold Dvořák (8 September 1841 – 1 May 1904) was of just about the same generation as Jirásek, a Czech composer who also had an interest in folk myths and song. After Bedřich Smetana, he was the second Czech composer to achieve worldwide recognition. Following Smetana's nationalist example, Dvořák frequently employed aspects, specifically rhythms, of the folk music of Moravia and his native Bohemia.

In 1896 once back in Prague, Dvořák wrote the symphonic poem The Water Sprite (Vodník), adapted from a poem of the same name in Karel Jaromír Erben’s celebrated collection, Kytice. You can listen to the opus on YouTube here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Tevnw2zRDk0
a film adaptation of Kytice from 2000
a comics version of the poem Komiksová kytice (Transmedialist 2016)
First two paragraphs adapted from Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anton%C3%ADn_Dvo%C5%99%C3%A1k)

links.context: We learned earlier about unchanging to in the expression to je… Regardless of the gender of a noun, the phrase stayed to je… (i.e. you never say ten je stůl, ta je holka). When we want to say those are… we just say to jsou… (and not ty jsou stoly, ty jsou holky)

paragraphs.text: ***Notice that the forms for masculine INanimate, feminine, and neuter are all the same in the plural, only the animate forms differ from them (MA moji, tvoji, naši, vaši instead of moje, tvoje, naše, vaše for MI, F, N.***
paragraphs.heading: 1.10 - Possessive Pronouns - Plural Forms
paragraphs.nextText: In order to make můj kamarád ‘my friend’ plural, i.e. ‘my friends’, you will not only make the word kamarád plural, but also use the plural form of the possessive pronoun. The plural form will be moji kamarádi ‘my friends’. The plural of vaše kniha ‘your book’ will be vaše knihy ‘your books’.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.10 - Possessive Pronouns - Plural Forms
jejich

In order to make můj kamarád ‘my friend’ plural, i.e. ‘my friends’, you will not only make the word kamarád plural, but also use the plural form of the possessive pronoun. The plural form will be moji kamarádi ‘my friends’. The plural of vaše kniha ‘your book’ will be vaše knihy ‘your books’.

links.context: A version of the Old Czech Legends was adapted into a stop motion feature length puppet film by the great Czech animator Jiří Trnka in 1956, and is available on YouTube here.

paragraphs.text: 1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
paragraphs.heading: 1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
paragraphs.nextText: Alois Jirásek (August 23, 1851, Hronov, Kingdom of Bohemia – March 12, 1930, Prague, Czechoslovakia) was a Czech writer, author of historical novels and plays. Jirásek was a high school history teacher in Litomyšl and later in Prague until his retirement in 1909. He wrote a series of historical novels imbued with faith in his nation and in progress toward freedom and justice and was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1918, 1919, 1921 and 1930.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
Alois Jirásek (August 23, 1851, Hronov, Kingdom of Bohemia – March 12, 1930, Prague, Czechoslovakia) was a Czech writer, author of historical novels and plays. Jirásek was a high school history teacher in Litomyšl and later in Prague until his retirement in 1909. He wrote a series of historical novels imbued with faith in his nation and in progress toward freedom and justice and was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1918, 1919, 1921 and 1930.

Jirásek was a high school history teacher in Litomyšl and later in Prague until his retirement in 1909. He wrote a series of historical novels imbued with faith in his nation and in progress toward freedom and justice and was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1918, 1919, 1921 and 1930.
paragraphs.heading: 1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
paragraphs.previousText: 1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
paragraphs.nextText: Jirásek is perhaps best known for his Old Czech Legends (Staré pověsti české) published in 1894, in which he recounts the popular Czech foundation myths.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.4 Alois Jirásek and the Old Czech Legends
Alois Jirásek (August 23, 1851, Hronov, Kingdom of Bohemia – March 12, 1930, Prague, Czechoslovakia) was a Czech writer, author of historical novels and plays. Jirásek was a high school history teacher in Litomyšl and later in Prague until his retirement in 1909. He wrote a series of historical novels imbued with faith in his nation and in progress toward freedom and justice and was nominated for the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1918, 1919, 1921 and 1930.
Jirásek is perhaps best known for his Old Czech Legends (Staré pověsti české) published in 1894, in which he recounts the popular Czech foundation myths.

You probably noticed that the definition for můj above is ‘my, mine’. While they are different words in English, Czech můj means both ‘my’ and ‘mine’. In other words, Czech doesn’t make a distinction between my and mine.

paragraphs.nextText: In English we typically put the form my right before the noun that is possessed, e.g. my phone, while we use the form mine after the verb ‘to be’, e.g. whose phone is this? It’s mine.

my vs. mine, your vs. yours, etc.
You probably noticed that the definition for můj above is ‘my, mine’. While they are different words in English, Czech můj means both ‘my’ and ‘mine’. In other words, Czech doesn’t make a distinction between my and mine.
In English we typically put the form my right before the noun that is possessed, e.g. my phone, while we use the form mine after the verb ‘to be’, e.g. whose phone is this? It’s mine.

paragraphs.heading: 1.11 - Odkud jste? ‘Where are you from?’
paragraphs.nextText: In this lesson we’re going to learn how to tell someone where you are from. This will require a few new forms, but for now we will focus primarily on learning how to say where you are from (and perhaps where a few people you know are from), as well as understanding when other people say where they are from.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.11 - Odkud jste? ‘Where are you from?’
In this lesson we’re going to learn how to tell someone where you are from. This will require a few new forms, but for now we will focus primarily on learning how to say where you are from (and perhaps where a few people you know are from), as well as understanding when other people say where they are from.

paragraphs.text: In this lesson we’re going to learn how to tell someone where you are from. This will require a few new forms, but for now we will focus primarily on learning how to say where you are from (and perhaps where a few people you know are from), as well as understanding when other people say where they are from.
paragraphs.heading: 1.11 - Odkud jste? ‘Where are you from?’
paragraphs.nextText: The question word odkud means ‘from where’. You use it to ask where someone is from, as in:
paragraphs.contextText:
1.11 - Odkud jste? ‘Where are you from?’
In this lesson we’re going to learn how to tell someone where you are from. This will require a few new forms, but for now we will focus primarily on learning how to say where you are from (and perhaps where a few people you know are from), as well as understanding when other people say where they are from.
The question word odkud means ‘from where’. You use it to ask where someone is from, as in:

paragraphs.heading: 1.6 - Possessive Pronouns
paragraphs.nextText: Possessive pronouns are words that mean ‘his’, ‘my’, ‘their’, etc. They are ways of showing possession.In Czech there are different forms of these words depending on the gender of the noun that follows them. You will have to learn the forms of these words and keep in mind the gender of the noun they go with when trying to decide which form to use.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.6 - Possessive Pronouns
Possessive pronouns are words that mean ‘his’, ‘my’, ‘their’, etc. They are ways of showing possession.In Czech there are different forms of these words depending on the gender of the noun that follows them. You will have to learn the forms of these words and keep in mind the gender of the noun they go with when trying to decide which form to use.

paragraphs.heading: 1.6 - Possessive Pronouns
paragraphs.nextText: For example, if you want to say ‘my friend’, you will say můj kamarád. In this instance, the form můj is used because the word kamarád in Czech is masculine. On the other hand, if you were talking about a female friend, then it would be moje kamarádka ‘my friend’ since the word kamarádka is feminine. If you wanted to say ‘my car, then it would be moje auto, since the word auto is neuter.
paragraphs.contextText:
1.6 - Possessive Pronouns
‘their, theirs’

Vowel Length

paragraphs.heading: Vowel Length
paragraphs.nextText: Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.
paragraphs.contextText:
Vowel Length
(listen)
Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.

paragraphs.text: Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.
paragraphs.heading: Vowel Length
paragraphs.contextText:
Vowel Length
Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.
Short Vowel

paragraphs.heading: Vowel Length
paragraphs.previousText: Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.
paragraphs.contextText:
Vowel Length
Vowels in Czech can be short or long. The vowels that you learned above are all short vowels. Czech has long vowels, which are marked by a čárka, as with  i.e. á, é, í, ý, ó, ú; or sometimes a kroužek, as in the case of ů. Long vowels are pronounced about twice as long as short vowels.

Special Pairs (i.e. diphthongs)

paragraphs.heading: Vowel Length
paragraphs.nextText: There are several pairs of vowels that get pronounced together in a way that resembles a new sound. In actuality, they are just the combination of the two individual sounds, but especially for English speakers it can be hard to predict what sound they will make.

Stress

paragraphs.heading: Stress
paragraphs.nextText: Stress in Czech is always on the first syllable . What this means is that the main “beat” of the word occurs on the very first syllable that you pronounce. Note that this is different from English, where stress can fall on any syllable (for example, in the word pretty stress is on the first syllable, in the word alike it is on the second syllable, in the word volunteer it’s on the third syllable). In Czech, stress placement is consistent and you will always find it on the first syllable. Keep this rule in mind as you practice pronunciation and listen back through the words above to practice this stress rule.
paragraphs.contextText:
Stress
fuj ‘yuck, gross!’
Stress in Czech is always on the first syllable . What this means is that the main “beat” of the word occurs on the very first syllable that you pronounce. Note that this is different from English, where stress can fall on any syllable (for example, in the word pretty stress is on the first syllable, in the word alike it is on the second syllable, in the word volunteer it’s on the third syllable). In Czech, stress placement is consistent and you will always find it on the first syllable. Keep this rule in mind as you practice pronunciation and listen back through the words above to practice this stress rule.
